Developing "AMAN OS upgrade" - a comprehensive multi-game entertainment system for Arduino Nano with 16x2 I2C LCD display. The system features a custom operating system interface with multiple interactive games, utilities, and animated UI elements inspired by Hajime no Ippo boxing anime. Key Features Implemented: Custom Boot Sequence: Slot machine animation spelling "AMAN OS" Animated UI: Dual-boxer fighting animation on idle screen, menu corners 6 Interactive Games: Runner (coin collection, progressive difficulty) Snake (classic gameplay adaptation) Reaction Test (reflex testing) Pong (vs CPU opponent) Memory Game (sequence repetition) Flappy Bird (3-lives system) Utility Applications: Stopwatch with lap timing Calculator (basic arithmetic) Dice Roller (D4-D20) Statistics Dashboard Settings Menu (difficulty, high score reset) Advanced Features: Single-button interface with smart click detection (click/hold/double-click) Progress bars for selections with countdown timers Pause menu system (2-second hold during gameplay) High score persistence via EEPROM Screensaver mode after 60 seconds idle Difficulty scaling system Technical Achievements: Optimized LCD refresh to minimize flickering Custom sprite creation for characters and animations Non-blocking input handling with interrupt-driven buttons State machine architecture for menu navigation Memory-efficient game logic within Arduino constraints EEPROM data management for persistent storage

This is a sensor packed device that features a GSR sensor and max30102 sensor that measure skin electrical conductivity (arousal), temperature, spo2, heart rate, HRV, and an accelerometer for filtering. The point of this is to build a tracker that isn't focused on fitness, but cognitive health tied to the nervous system. These sensors will be packed into a small flex PCB in a ring shape.. All of the circuitry has been tested in LT-Spice to ensure electrical correctness. The key point of this project is to have this ring focused on mental and cognitive health rather than fitness and physical health. So, the sensors send data to the Raytac nRF52840 module and the Raytac sends it to a mobile device via BLE. The data goes through a filter in the app, removing any outliers. Additionally, in the app there is a "Cognitive Score." The app interprets all the data and based off of an algorithm, it can give you a score out of 100 as well as some insights.

This project involves building a bus stopping bell buzzer similar to those used on public buses in Singapore. For the button mechanism, I used a 3D printed model that I found online and printed using a 3D printer. The printed button acts as the physical interface for the user to press, similar to the real stop buttons found on buses. When the button is pressed, the system triggers a buzzer sound that imitates the bus stopping bell. This project combines 3D printing, electronics, and programming to recreate a familiar real-world system.
